critic_raw:
ASSESSOR 1:
Verdict: critique_wins

The 12-point Recall@100 gap cannot be attributed to neural retrieval architecture because the two systems differ in supervision source (impression data with negatives vs. positive-only play events) and feature set (audio embeddings + skip behavior vs. play events only). The internal reviewers' question is exactly right. No intermediate baseline (impression-aware MF or content-aware shallow model) is included to isolate the architecture contribution.
defender_raw:
ASSESSOR 2:
Verdict: critique_wins

The supervision source mismatch is the primary issue — training on explicit negatives from impression data vs. positive-only signals is a fundamental difference in what the model learns. Even without neural architecture, a model trained on impression data with negatives would be expected to outperform positive-only matrix factorization on Recall@100. The paper cannot claim neural retrieval quality from this comparison.

ASSESSOR 3:
Verdict: critique_wins

Three compounding issues: supervision source mismatch, feature set mismatch, and no intermediate ablation baseline. All three are confirmed by the paper's own experimental details section. The paper explicitly acknowledges that 'reconstructing impression-level negatives for the baseline pipeline would have been too expensive' — this admission confirms the supervision mismatch. The Recall@100 gap conflates supervision richness, feature richness, and architecture.
adjudication_raw:
Unanimous critique_wins. All three assessors independently identify the same three issues and reach the same verdict. The supervision mismatch in particular is explicitly acknowledged in the paper. The paper's own framing as evidence of 'clear improvement in candidate generation quality' is not supported by the comparison design.

Verdict: critique_wins.
